characters.blurb: Led the Senones that crushed Rome at the Allia and held the city for ransom until Camillus returned as dictator.
Rome’s rich rival across the Tiber. Camillus took it after a long siege—by tunnel into Juno’s temple, in the famous tale.

characters.relation: Goddess brought from Veii to Rome
characters.blurb: Juno Regina nodded (they say) when asked to go to Rome; Camillus’s evocatio made her a Roman goddess.
Heard the sacred geese and held the Tarpeian rock against the Gauls; later courted the debtors and fell as a demagogue.
Title of Marcus Manlius after defending the Capitol—glory that later curdled into sedition against Camillus’s order.
When their schoolmaster betrayed the boys to Camillus, he returned the traitor unbound and won the city by justice.
Marcus Furius Camillus—five times dictator—the man Plutarch paints as a second Romulus after the Gallic sack.
After the sack some urged abandoning Rome; Camillus argued like a re-founder that the site itself was sacred.
Stream where the Gauls destroyed the Roman army on a dies ater still marked in the calendar.
Etruscan-facing town taken and retaken in the wars; Camillus restored it in a single day’s hard fighting.

characters.relation: City of his exile; base of return
characters.blurb: Camillus was living in exile there when the Gauls came; from Ardea he gathered strength to relieve Rome.
Carried word through the enemy to the Capitol—and brought the Senate’s recall of Camillus as dictator.
Plebeian who reported a divine voice in the Via Nova foretelling the Gallic onset—ignored until too late.

characters.relation: Clan prominent in the Veientine war
characters.blurb: Their private war at the Cremera and later commands frame the generation Camillus dominated.
Tribune or commander who weighed the thousand pounds of gold before Camillus forbade the shameful bargain.
Pressed Rome while she was weak; Camillus’s dictatorships repeatedly broke their invasions.
Fought beside Volscians in the post-Gallic wars Camillus was called to master.
Broader Etruscan front—Capena, Falerii, and others—against which Camillus earned his early fame.
Several Lucii share Camillus’s Fasti as consular tribunes and fellow generals in the long Veientine struggle.
Named among Camillus’s awkward colleagues when politics cut short a command or triumph.

characters.relation: Peaceful king invoked after the sack
characters.blurb: When Romans debated moving to Veii, Camillus’s speech leaned on Numa’s religious founding of the city’s sites.

characters.relation: Town whose quarrel brought the Gauls
characters.blurb: Etruscan city that invited Roman envoys against the Gauls—and so drew Brennus south on Rome.
Lucumo wronged at Clusium who guided the Gallic mercenaries into Etruria—and toward Rome.

characters.relation: Tribune of the people; reforming land-law agitator
characters.blurb: Raised a great sedition demanding that no one hold more than five hundred acres; the senate needed a dictator to pass the law, then caught Stolo himself violating it.

characters.relation: Envoy and soldier in the Gallic crisis
characters.blurb: Fabius Ambustus was one of the envoys who visited Delphi before the Veii siege; Quintus Ambustus, well-mounted, charged a Gaul in the hill skirmish that drew the Senones south.

characters.relation: Devout citizen who rescued the Vestal Virgins
characters.blurb: Traveling by cart with his family during the Gallic rout, he made his own family walk so the Vestals and their sacred objects could ride to safety at Caere.

characters.relation: Senator who struck a Gaul with his staff
characters.blurb: Old senator sitting still in his chair in the Forum when the Gauls entered; struck a Gaul who stroked his beard, and was the first to die in the sack of the city.

characters.relation: Handmaid who saved Rome from the Latins by deception
characters.blurb: Called Philotis or Tutula, this slave-woman persuaded the magistrates to send her and other handmaids disguised as free women into the Latin camp; she lit the signal fire.

characters.relation: Magistrate who voted first on moving to Veii
characters.blurb: Senior officer whose turn it was to speak first when Rome debated abandoning the burned city for Veii; a centurion’s chance command interrupted him as a divine sign.

characters.relation: Philosopher-historian who recorded the Gallic sack in Greece
characters.blurb: Wrote of a great army from the far west destroying a Greek city called Rome—Plutarch’s earliest Greek evidence for the sack, though he calls Heraclides high-flown.

characters.relation: Trojan ancestor invoked in the debate on Rome’s site
characters.blurb: Plutarch notes that the Palladium and Samothracian images were said to have come with Aeneas to Italy—relics stored in the inmost shrine whose rites Numa founded.

characters.relation: Macedonian king compared to Camillus in hypothetical match
characters.blurb: Plutarch entertains the conjecture of what would have happened had Alexander marched west and met Rome—Camillus’s Rome is the implied Roman champion.

locations.relation: City Camillus served, lost, and restored
locations.blurb: Centre of every phase of his career—triumph, exile, recall, and five dictatorships that rebuilt the city after the Gauls.

locations.relation: Etruscan rival taken after a ten-year siege
locations.blurb: Head city of Tuscany; Camillus crept in by tunnel beneath Juno’s temple, sacked it, and solemnly translated the goddess to Rome.
He besieged Falerii and won it without a fight by returning the boys their schoolmaster had betrayed—justice that earned a willing surrender.

locations.relation: Exile refuge turned rallying base against the Gauls
locations.blurb: Camillus sojourned in exile at Ardea when the Gauls came; he rallied its people, routed a Gallic foraging party, and gathered the army that relieved Rome.

locations.relation: Latin ally rescued from Etruscan siege
locations.blurb: After driving the Gauls from Rome, Camillus marched to Sutrium’s relief—arriving to find the people stripped and destitute, then retaking the city in a single day’s hard fighting.

locations.relation: Latin hill-town whose revolt Camillus suppressed by clemency
locations.blurb: Tasked with reducing the Tusculan revolt, he found the city at outward peace and chose intercession over punishment, earning the Tusculans Roman citizenship.

locations.relation: River of his last battle against the Gauls
locations.blurb: Near the river Anio he lured the plunder-heavy Gauls onto broken ground, concealed most of his army in the folds of a hill, and drove them from Italy for the last time.

locations.relation: River where Rome’s army was destroyed before Camillus’s recall
locations.blurb: The Allia disaster—Rome’s darkest day—fell on the same calendar date as the earlier Cremera catastrophe of the Fabii, fixing it forever as a day of ill omen.

locations.relation: Etruscan city whose appeal drew the Gauls south toward Rome
locations.blurb: Clusium called for Roman envoys against Gallic pressure; when the Fabii ambassadors fought beside the besieged, Brennus used it as his pretext to march on Rome.

locations.relation: Oracle consulted about the Alban lake prodigy
locations.blurb: The senate sent Fabius Ambustus and two others to Delphi when the Alban lake rose without cause; the oracle confirmed the ten-year siege of Veii was nearing its end.

locations.relation: Etruscan town allied with the Faliscans in raids on Roman territory
locations.blurb: The Capenates raided in concert with the Faliscans while Camillus was occupied at Veii, forcing him to suppress both peoples once the great siege was won.

locations.relation: Latin city that submitted voluntarily after the Anio victory
locations.blurb: After the Anio rout the Velitrani surrendered without a fight—Plutarch calls it a mere accessory to Camillus’s final great campaign.
